key_questions: Seasonal (tree/grass/ragweed) or perennial (dust mites, pet dander, mold)?
Duration and timing of symptoms — morning vs throughout day?
Current medications including nasal decongestants (rhinitis medicamentosa risk)?
red_flags: Unilateral nasal obstruction or bleeding → polyp or malignancy
Facial pain/pressure + fever + purulent discharge → sinusitis
Symptoms not responding to adequate trial of intranasal corticosteroids

first_line.name: Intranasal Corticosteroid
first_line.drugs: Fluticasone (Flonase) 2 sprays each nostril OD | Mometasone (Nasonex) 2 sprays each nostril OD | Budesonide (Rhinocort)
first_line.notes: Most effective pharmacotherapy for allergic rhinitis — superior to antihistamines for congestion and nasal symptoms. Takes 1–2 weeks for full effect. Spray toward outer ear, not nasal septum (prevents septum perforation). Can be used long-term.
first_line.preferred_when: Persistent symptoms or predominantly nasal congestion

first_line.name: Oral Non-Sedating Antihistamine
first_line.drugs: Cetirizine (Reactine) 10 mg OD | Loratadine (Claritin) 10 mg OD | Fexofenadine (Allegra) 120–180 mg OD
first_line.notes: First-line for mild-intermittent symptoms or sneezing/itch predominance. Non-sedating (second-generation). Take daily for seasonal allergies (continuous > PRN). Cetirizine: mildly sedating in some — take at night.
first_line.preferred_when: Itching, sneezing, watery eyes as predominant symptoms

second_line.name: Intranasal Antihistamine
second_line.drugs: Azelastine (Astelin) nasal spray 1–2 sprays each nostril BID
second_line.notes: Faster onset than oral antihistamines. Useful for breakthrough symptoms or when INCS insufficient.

second_line.name: Oral Decongestant (short-term only)
second_line.notes: For acute nasal congestion only — do NOT use >5 days (rebound congestion). Avoid in hypertension, hyperthyroidism, BPH, MAOIs. Behind pharmacy counter (pseudoephedrine).

second_line.name: Montelukast (Singulair)
second_line.notes: Useful for allergic rhinitis + asthma combination. FDA/Health Canada BBW for neuropsychiatric events — reserve for clear indication.

non_pharm: Allergen avoidance: dust mite covers, HEPA filters, minimize outdoor exposure during high pollen
Nasal saline irrigation (Neti pot, NeilMed) — reduces symptoms and medication need
Monitor local pollen counts (Weather Network pollen forecast)
Close windows during high pollen season, use air conditioning
treatment.refer_when: Severe uncontrolled symptoms despite adequate pharmacotherapy; suspected polyps; immunotherapy consideration; suspected sinusitis or anatomical obstruction
